title: Check yourself: content
questions.0.q: You want a sub-heading, but <h2> looks too small to you. What do you do?
questions.0.options.1: Keep <h2> and enlarge it later with CSS
questions.0.options.2:
Use a

and make it bold
questions.0.options.3: Use <h1>, but with a smaller font
questions.0.hint: Ask what the heading level is for — the size, or something else?
questions.0.explain: The heading level is about meaning, not size. If it is a subsection — it is an <h2>, however small it looks. The size is fixed with CSS. The meaning cannot be fixed with anything.
questions.1.q: You make a list of your favourite films. Their order means nothing. Which do you pick?
questions.1.options.1: <ul> — because the order does not matter
questions.1.options.3: Does not matter — both are lists
questions.1.hint: What does a numbered list CLAIM about the things inside it?
questions.1.explain: Numbers say "this is first, this is second". If the order is arbitrary, you are lying to the reader. The test is simple: swap two items. If nothing changes — <ul>.
questions.2.q: You forget to close a <strong> halfway down the page. What happens?
questions.2.options.3: Nothing — <strong> does not have to be closed
questions.2.hint: You said where the important part begins. Did you ever say where it ends?
questions.2.explain: You said "the important part starts here" and never said where it ends. The browser assumes the important part is everything until the end. It does not complain — it just carries on.
questions.3.q: You wonder why you should use <strong> instead of just bold letters with CSS. A colleague says: "because the screen reader says it in a firmer voice". Are they right?
questions.3.options.0: Yes — that is exactly the difference between <strong> and CSS
questions.3.options.1: No — the reader is silent about both. <strong> records WHY, not how it sounds
questions.3.options.3: No — <strong> is not about importance at all
questions.3.hint: This is the most repeated claim about <strong> on the web. Check it before you believe it.
questions.3.explain: Neither JAWS, nor NVDA, nor VoiceOver say anything about <strong>. NVDA did it once and stopped — emphasis is so overused on the web that the noise became unbearable. So why the tag? Because it records the MEANING: this is important. For you in six months, for the machine processing the text, for the day the readers grow up. And the consequence few will tell you: since the reader stays silent, do not rely on bold to carry importance. Say it in words too.
